content.text: Argument Generation

When the model decides to call a tool, it generates the argument values by reading the schema and extracting information from the conversation. The quality of the generated arguments depends directly on how clearly the schema describes each parameter — what format is expected, where the value comes from, and what to do when it is absent.

attrs.title: User message → Model reasoning → Generated arguments

content.text: The model reads parameter descriptions as instructions, not just documentation.

Telling it the expected format ('YYYY-MM-DD'), the normalisation rule ('use uppercase IATA codes'), or the fallback behaviour ('ask the user if not provided') changes what it generates — reliably and immediately.
Observing Generated Arguments
The model constructs the
input
dict by matching schema fields to values in the conversation. Required fields are extracted from the user message or conversation history; optional fields use their
default
values when not mentioned:
Field
Source
Notes
origin
User message ('London')
Normalised to IATA code per description
destination
User message ('Paris')
Normalised to IATA code per description
date
User message ('June 15th')
Converted to YYYY-MM-DD per description
passengers
User message ('two people')
Extracted number
cabin_class
Not mentioned → default
Schema default 'economy' applied
Type Coercion and Normalisation

The model applies intelligent coercion: it converts natural language values to the schema's expected type and format. Descriptions with explicit examples and format instructions produce more reliable normalisation:
Include format examples in descriptions: 'e.g. AAPL', 'e.g. 2024-01-15'
For entity names, specify the canonical form: 'use the official company ticker symbol'
For dates, state the format explicitly: 'ISO 8601, YYYY-MM-DD'
For numbers, state units if ambiguous: 'temperature in degrees Celsius'
Drawing Arguments from Context
The model reads the entire conversation history when generating arguments. Values mentioned in earlier turns are available for later tool calls without the user repeating them:

This context-awareness is powerful but requires care: if the same field appears multiple times with different values across turns (e.g., the user corrected an earlier mistake), the model should use the most recent value. The description can reinforce this: 'use the order ID most recently mentioned in the conversation'.
Missing Required Arguments
When a required argument cannot be found in the conversation, the model should ask the user rather than guess. The description is the right place to specify this fallback behaviour:

This works because the model reads descriptions as behaviour instructions. Adding 'If you do not know X, ask the user for it before calling this tool' is a reliable way to prevent hallucinated argument values for critical fields like emails, IDs, or account numbers.
Validating Generated Arguments
Even with a good schema, it is worth checking the generated
block.input
before executing the tool. A lightweight validation function catches missing required fields and type mismatches before they cause confusing runtime errors:
Enum-Guided Normalisation
Enum fields let the model normalise varied user phrasing to a fixed set of values. Pair the enum with a description that maps common user terms to specific enum members:
The model will always choose from the enum values — it will never generate an unlisted value
Use the description to specify how to map ambiguous input: 'urgent' → critical, 'normal' → medium
Enums are more reliable than open string fields for fields with known valid values
Avoid enum lists longer than 8-10 members — larger sets make it harder for the model to pick correctly
Nested and Array Arguments

The model constructs nested objects and arrays from natural language when the schema specifies them. Provide clear descriptions on nested properties — the model must understand both the outer structure and the inner field requirements:
For array arguments, the model infers the number of items from the user's message (two attendees → two objects in the array). Descriptions like 'List of attendees' combined with clear
items
schemas produce well-formed arrays consistently.
Pre-filling Arguments with Server-Side Context

Some arguments are known server-side (user ID, session token, account email) and should never come from the model. Inject these as context in the user message so the model uses the authoritative value rather than asking the user or constructing one:
Inject server-known values as bracketed context at the start of the user message
This pattern prevents the model from asking for values you already have
It also prevents hallucinated IDs — the model uses what you provide, not what it guesses
Never put secrets (API keys, passwords) in user messages — only pass non-sensitive identifiers
Common Argument Generation Mistakes

Most argument quality problems trace back to schema descriptions that are too vague or that fail to specify the expected format and fallback behaviour. Here are the most frequent issues and their fixes:
Mistake
Cause
Fix
Model invents an ID it cannot know
Description doesn't say to ask the user
Add 'Ask for it if not provided' to description
Date in wrong format or year
No format specified in description
State 'YYYY-MM-DD' and anchor relative dates
Boolean sent as string 'true'
Parameter type set to string instead of boolean
Use type: boolean in the schema
Model repeats stale value from earlier turn
User corrected it but description ambiguous
Add 'use the most recently mentioned value'
Optional field always sent with wrong default
Default doesn't match real intent
Add 'default is X unless user specifies otherwise'
Summary
Argument generation is driven by the schema and conversation context. The model extracts required argument values from the user message and conversation history, applies
default
values for optional fields, and normalises values to the expected format using instructions in the description. Explicit format examples (
'e.g. YYYY-MM-DD'
), normalisation rules, and fallback instructions ('ask the user if not provided') produce reliable argument generation. Validate
block.input
before executing the tool to catch residual errors before they reach your application logic.
